planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; false sarsaparilla is a vigorous twining vine or sprawling shrub that blooms in late winter and early spring (July–September in Australia; January–April in zone 9) when little else is flowering. Several selections are available: 'Happy Wanderer' (vigorous vine with deep purple flowers), 'Mini Haha' (compact shrub form), white-flowered and pink-flowered forms. Tolerates moderate drought once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; false sarsaparilla is one of the most widely grown and adaptable Australian native ornamentals outside its homeland — a vigorous twining vine or sprawling groundcover shrub that produces in late winter and early spring a spectacular, prolific display of small but densely clustered, typical sweet pea-family flowers in vivid, rich, deep purple-violet with a distinctive small, contrasting, white-and-yellow mark at the center of each standard petal. The flowers are borne in long, pendant or arching racemes of 20–40 individual blossoms, and a plant in full late-winter bloom — covered in masses of deep-purple flower racemes against the dark, leathery, lance-shaped evergreen leaves — is a very striking sight in the winter-spring garden. The bloom period (late winter to early spring) provides vivid purple color at a time when the garden most needs it, and the flowers are attractive to honeyeating birds and bees. The form 'Happy Wanderer' is among the most commonly available and most vigorous cultivars. Tolerates considerable neglect once established, making it an ideal low-maintenance vine for fences, banks, and ground-covering in zones 8–11.

common_name: Dusky Coral Pea (Running Postman Coral Pea)

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; dusky coral pea is a fast-growing, vigorous vine or trailing groundcover suitable for difficult, sunny, dry banks and slopes where few other flowering vines would survive. It blooms mainly in spring but sporadically year-round in mild climates. The dense, trailing stems with large, three-parted, dark-green leaves provide excellent soil erosion control. Very tolerant of coastal exposure and poor soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; dusky coral pea is a robust, vigorous, trailing or twining Australian native legume from the coastal scrub and dry forests of eastern Australia — producing in spring a succession of the distinctive Kennedia flower form: a typical sweet-pea-family flower with a broad, curved, deep dusky-red to dark coral-red to brick-red standard petal and smaller, darker, often almost blood-red wing and keel petals, the whole flower a rich, warm, dusky-red combination. The flowers are borne in clusters of 2–4 in the leaf axils, scattered throughout the vine over the spring bloom period. The plant itself is vigorous and fast-growing — potentially reaching considerable size as a vine or sprawling, mounding groundcover — with large, trifoliate, deep-green, somewhat coarse-textured leaves. Excellent for covering difficult, dry, sunny, rocky banks and slopes where its drought tolerance, fast growth, and nitrogen-fixing root nodules make it both ornamental and functionally valuable. The trailing stems root where they contact soil, spreading into an increasingly dense groundcover. An excellent, low-maintenance, soil-stabilizing ornamental for zone 9 coastal and inland slopes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; heart-leaf flame pea is native to the sandy soils of southwestern Western Australia where it grows in open woodland and heath. It blooms most heavily in late winter and spring. The small leaves with holly-like spiny-toothed margins and the brilliantly bicolored flowers (orange-red with purple/yellow) make it immediately recognizable. Relatively short-lived (5–8 years) but self-seeds in sandy, well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; heart-leaf flame pea is a small Australian shrub of exceptional flower brilliance — producing in late winter and spring such an abundance of vivid, small, sweet pea-family flowers that a plant in full bloom appears entirely engulfed in a smothering cloak of the most vivid bicolor flowers: the standard petal brilliant orange-red to flame-red, the keel and wing petals a contrasting purple to deep violet with a small yellow blotch at the base, the combination of orange, purple, and yellow creating an intense, extravagant, tropical-colored display of remarkable saturation and vibrancy for such a small shrub. The small, heart-shaped to ovate leaves with holly-like, spiny-toothed margins (giving both the common name 'holly flame pea' and the species name cordatum = heart-shaped) provide an attractive, textured foliage background year-round. Native to the sandy soils of the Stirling Ranges and coastal heath of southwestern Western Australia, it requires the lean, well-drained soil conditions of its native habitat to thrive. One of the most brilliantly colored small shrubs available for the zone 9 garden.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; banjine is native to the coastal heaths and scrubs of southwestern Western Australia where it receives high rainfall but excellent drainage. It blooms in spring, producing dense, rounded heads of rich rose-pink tubular flowers. It does not tolerate root disturbance well — plant in final position without repotting. Sensitive to overwatering and poor drainage.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; banjine (or rice flower) is a neat, compact, mounding Australian shrub from the coastal heathlands of southwestern Western Australia — producing in spring dense, rounded terminal clusters of many small, tubular, four-petaled flowers in a rich, warm, rosy-pink to deep pink, each small flower head about an inch across and the clusters collectively covering the plant in an abundant pink display. The common name 'rice flower' comes from the appearance of the dense, rounded, tightly packed flower clusters resembling a cluster of tiny rice grains dipped in pink. The compact, neat, naturally mounding growth habit and glossy, small, dark-green leaves provide attractive structure year-round, and the plant maintains a tidy, ornamental character without significant pruning. It is in the Thymelaeaceae family (the same family as daphne), and like daphne, it has a reputation for sensitivity to site conditions — it resents transplanting, requires excellent drainage, and is unforgiving of waterlogged conditions. In the right position in zones 9–11 Mediterranean-climate or warm coastal gardens, however, it is a rewarding, long-lived, compact ornamental shrub.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; golden featherflower is one of the most difficult of all Western Australian natives to cultivate outside its native habitat — it requires perfect drainage, lean soil, and the precise conditions of the sandy coastal plains of the Perth area. Grafted plants on Darwinia or Calytrix rootstocks have improved success. It is the floral emblem of Western Australia's capital region. Not for beginners; a challenge-and-reward plant for dedicated native plant enthusiasts.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; golden featherflower is one of the most spectacular of all the Australian native shrubs in bloom — a Western Australian coastal heath specialist from the sandy coastal plains around Perth (where it is so abundant in spring that it colors whole hillsides brilliant yellow-gold, and is the informal floral symbol of the Perth region) that produces in late winter and spring an astonishing, total-coverage, smothering mass of tiny (0.5 inch), yellow to orange-yellow, feathery-edged flowers covering every stem and twig of the plant in a dense, almost furry, golden-yellow coating. The 'feather' texture of the flowers — each individual flower with finely fringed, feathery-textured petals — gives the genus its common name (Verticordia, meaning 'turning hearts', is sometimes called the featherflower genus), and the density of the yellow flowers on a mature plant in full bloom creates an effect like a shrub dipped in gold or covered in bright-yellow powder. It is considered one of the most spectacular and beautiful spring-flowering shrubs of Australia's west coast. The difficulty of cultivation outside its native sandy coastal habitat is well known — it is considered one of the 'holy grail' plants of Australian horticulture, requiring very specific conditions.
